review.method: Item-by-item review of the stored June 2024 Paper 2 official question wording, primary answer chain and imported mark-scheme rows against the local AQA Biology curriculum; no prompt classifier or model call.
review.boundary: Questions map only where the official item evidence fixes a learner-selectable Biology chapter. Generic arithmetic or graph construction with incidental topic context is explicitly withheld.

question.promptText: What is a mutation?
question.selfContainedPromptText: Evolution of new species occurs by mutation and natural selection. What is a mutation?

primaryAnswerChain.title: Mutation
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: DNA/chromosome -> sequence/number change
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Mutation means an alteration in genetic material.

markSchemeItems.text: A change in DNA, the base code or nucleotide sequence, a base in DNA, a gene or allele, part of a chromosome, the number of chromosomes, or the genetic code/material.
Ignore genetic information or variation; ignore references to amino acids or proteins.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.6 Inheritance, variation and evolution.

question.promptText: Describe the process of natural selection.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Evolution of new species occurs by mutation and natural selection. Describe the process of natural selection.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Natural selection
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: variation -> better adapted -> survive -> reproduce -> alleles inherited
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Selection links variation to inherited reproductive success.

markSchemeItems.text: Variation exists between members of a species.
Better-adapted individuals survive (survival of the fittest).
The better-adapted individuals or survivors reproduce.
They pass on favourable alleles, genes or mutations.
Award any three points. Ignore mutation as a substitute for variation and ignore passing on unspecified genetic material, chromosomes or characteristics.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.6 Inheritance, variation and evolution.

question.promptText: Which scientists suggested the theory of evolution by natural selection? Tick (✓) one box.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Which scientists suggested the theory of evolution by natural selection? Tick (✓) one box.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Theory credit
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: natural-selection theory -> joint originators
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Joint credit is the recall cue.

markSchemeItems.text: Alfred Wallace and Charles Darwin.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.6 Inheritance, variation and evolution.

question.promptText: Explain how having yellow and black markings helps the hoverfly survive.
question.selfContainedPromptText: The hoverfly and the wasp are insects with bright yellow and black markings. Figure 1 shows a hoverfly and a wasp. The wasp has a sting to defend itself against predators. The hoverfly does not have a sting. Hoverflies and wasps live in the same habitat. Explain how having yellow and black markings helps the hoverfly survive.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Protective mimicry
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: warning-pattern mimic -> predator avoids sting -> mimic not attacked
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: A harmless mimic gains the defended model’s protection.

markSchemeItems.text: The hoverfly looks like a wasp; allow that the pattern of their markings is similar.
A predator avoids wasps so that it does not get stung.
Therefore the predator does not attack or eat the hoverfly.
Do not credit merely saying the predator thinks the hoverfly is a wasp; the avoidance and survival consequence are needed.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.6 Inheritance, variation and evolution.

question.promptText: Explain why most of the dead remains of plants in layer B do not decay.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Peat bogs are estimated to contain twice as much carbon as all the world's forests. Figure 2 shows a section through part of a peat bog. Layer A contains a lot of air. Layer B contains the dead remains of plants, has a low pH, contains very little oxygen, and contains carbon dioxide and methane. Explain why most of the dead remains of plants in layer B do not decay.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Decay limits
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: low oxygen/low pH -> respiration/enzyme route -> less energy/reaction -> decomposers limited
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Either environmental route ends in reduced decomposition.

markSchemeItems.text: There is too little oxygen for aerobic respiration.
Therefore less or no energy is released.
This limits microorganisms, bacteria, fungi, microbes or decomposers.
Alternatively, the low pH denatures enzymes or reduces enzyme activity.
Therefore there are fewer or no chemical reactions, metabolism or respiration, or less/no energy is released.
This limits microorganisms, bacteria, fungi, microbes or decomposers.
Use either the low-oxygen route or the low-pH route. Do not accept energy being produced, made or created.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.7 Ecology.

question.promptText: The peat bog in Figure 2 is a stable community. The moss produces biomass at a rate of 340 g/m²/year. What is the approximate biomass of the moss that becomes biomass in primary consumers? Tick (✓) one box.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 2 shows a section through a peat bog containing moss and other plants. The peat bog in Figure 2 is a stable community. The moss produces biomass at a rate of 340 g/m²/year. What is the approximate biomass of the moss that becomes biomass in primary consumers? Tick (✓) one box.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Trophic transfer
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: producer biomass -> transfer fraction -> consumer biomass
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Only a fraction reaches the next trophic level.

markSchemeItems.text: 34 g/m²/year.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.7 Ecology.

question.promptText: What substance can the sundew plant make using the phosphates? Tick (✓) one box.
question.selfContainedPromptText: The sundew plant shown in Figure 2 has leaves with sticky hairs that trap and digest insects. Digestion of the insects releases phosphates and simple compounds of nitrogen that are used by the sundew plant. What substance can the sundew plant make using the phosphates? Tick (✓) one box.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Phosphate role
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: phosphate compounds -> genetic material
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Phosphate supports genetic-material synthesis.

markSchemeItems.text: DNA.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.7 Ecology.

question.promptText: What substance can the sundew plant make using the nitrogen? Tick (✓) one box.
question.selfContainedPromptText: The sundew plant shown in Figure 2 has leaves with sticky hairs that trap and digest insects. Digestion of the insects releases phosphates and simple compounds of nitrogen that are used by the sundew plant. What substance can the sundew plant make using the nitrogen? Tick (✓) one box.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Nitrogen role
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: nitrogen compounds -> amino-acid synthesis
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Nitrogen feeds amino-acid and biomolecule production.

markSchemeItems.text: Protein.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.7 Ecology.

question.promptText: Explain how the destruction of peat bogs and the use of peat affects the temperature of the Earth's atmosphere.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Humans have destroyed large areas of peat bog to collect peat. The peat provides fuel and provides compost for gardeners to use. The peat comes from layer B in Figure 2. Layer B contains the dead remains of plants, has a low pH, contains very little oxygen, and contains carbon dioxide and methane. Figure 3 shows the removal of peat from a peat bog: peat is dug out and cut into 'bricks' that are left to dry. Explain how the destruction of peat bogs and the use of peat affects the temperature of the Earth's atmosphere.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Peat warming
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: peat carbon disturbed -> greenhouse gases rise -> less carbon uptake -> heat trapped -> temperature rises
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Stored carbon loss strengthens greenhouse warming.

markSchemeItems.text: The temperature increases (global warming; heat energy is trapped).
Carbon dioxide is released from the peat bog.
Carbon dioxide is produced by burning or decay of peat; alternatively, fewer plants take in carbon dioxide for photosynthesis.
Methane is released from the peat bog.
For the temperature point, ignore an unlinked reference to greenhouse gases. Ignore methane said to be released from burning or decay of peat.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.7 Ecology.

question.promptText: Suggest one improvement to the method.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Frogs lay their eggs in water and the eggs hatch as tadpoles. Students investigated the number of tadpoles in a pond for 8 weeks. They collected 10 dm³ of pond water in a bucket, counted the tadpoles, returned them to the pond, repeated this another three times in different parts of the pond, and repeated the sampling at intervals for 8 weeks. Suggest one improvement to the method.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Sampling improvement
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: sampling design -> frequency/volume/placement -> concrete improvement
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Improvements must change the sampling procedure.

markSchemeItems.text: Collect more samples each time.
Collect samples more frequently; an appropriate suggested time interval is allowed.
Use a bigger bucket or sample.
Do not return tadpoles until after the fourth sample, or use another method that avoids double counting.
Sample at the same time of day.
Randomise the collecting positions.
Collect at a range of depths.
Use standardised sweeps with a net instead of a bucket.
Award any one accepted improvement.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.7 Ecology.

question.promptText: After 4 weeks many of the tadpoles had died. Suggest two reasons why the tadpoles died.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Students investigated the number of tadpoles in a pond for 8 weeks. After 0 weeks no more tadpoles hatched, and after 4 weeks many of the tadpoles had died. Suggest two reasons why the tadpoles died.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Mortality risks
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: pond mortality -> disease/predation/resources/conditions -> choose two
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Distinct, qualified hazards score independently.

markSchemeItems.text: Disease or named pathogens.
Being eaten or predators.
Lack of food; allow competition for food.
Low oxygen concentration in the water; allow eutrophication.
A change in temperature.
A change in pH.
Some of the pond dried out; allow lack of space.
A toxic chemical, including a named example such as sewage or fertiliser.
Award any two. Ignore unqualified competition, pollution or waste.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.7 Ecology.

question.promptText: Give the reason why the student placed both sets of apparatus in the dark.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A student investigated the effect of gravity on the growth of bean seedlings. Figure 6 shows apparatus A, which was stationary, and apparatus B, which rotated slowly. The student left both sets of apparatus in a dark cupboard for 24 hours. Give the reason why the student placed both sets of apparatus in the dark.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Light control
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: darkness -> no phototropism -> gravity isolated
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Darkness removes the light-direction cue.

markSchemeItems.text: To prevent the direction of light affecting the results or root growth (prevent phototropism), so that only gravity affects the results/growth.
Ignore references only to the shoot or merely saying this is a control variable.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: What are two reasons for surrounding the seedlings with damp blotting paper? Tick (✓) two boxes.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A student investigated the effect of gravity on bean seedlings using the stationary apparatus A and slowly rotating apparatus B shown in Figure 6. In both sets of apparatus, the seedlings were surrounded by damp blotting paper. What are two reasons for surrounding the seedlings with damp blotting paper? Tick (✓) two boxes.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Moisture control
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: damp all around -> no moisture direction -> water supplied
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Moisture removes bias and supplies growth water.

markSchemeItems.text: To prevent water affecting the direction of root growth.
To provide enough water for root growth.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: Apparatus B is a control. Explain why apparatus B is needed.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A student investigated the effect of gravity on the growth of bean seedlings. Figure 6 shows stationary apparatus A and apparatus B, which rotates slowly. Both were kept in a dark cupboard for 24 hours. Apparatus B is a control. Explain why apparatus B is needed.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Rotating control
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: rotating control -> direction averaged -> compare with stationary -> gravity isolated
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Rotation removes a fixed gravity direction.

markSchemeItems.text: Apparatus B provides a comparison with apparatus A (allows the difference to be seen).
It shows that gravity caused the results in apparatus A, because gravity acts equally in all directions in B or the one-sided effect of gravity is cancelled out.
Ignore an explanation only in terms of auxin, and do not credit saying that gravity itself is cancelled out.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: Complete Figure 7 to show: the appearance of the root after 24 hours; the ink marks on the root after 24 hours.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A student investigated gravity and bean-seedling growth. The student put ink marks along each root, as shown in Figure 5. Apparatus A in Figure 6 was stationary, with the seedlings initially horizontal, and was kept in a dark cupboard for 24 hours. Figure 7 shows one seedling from apparatus A at the start and an incomplete drawing after 24 hours. Complete Figure 7 to show the appearance of the root and the ink marks after 24 hours.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Root gravitropism
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: gravity response -> root bends down -> root extends -> growth-zone spacing
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Bending and elongation occur in the growth region.

markSchemeItems.text: Draw the root bending downwards.
Draw the root longer than it was at the start.
Show the ink marks spread out in the bent region.
Do not accept ink marks spread out before the bend.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: Describe how a root from apparatus B would look different from the root you drew in Question 04.4.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 6 shows apparatus A, which is stationary, and apparatus B, which rotates slowly; both were kept in the dark for 24 hours. Figure 7 is the source drawing in Question 04.4 for completing the root of a seedling from apparatus A after 24 hours. Describe how a root from apparatus B would look different from that apparatus A root.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Rotation outcome
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: direction averaged -> no gravitropic bend -> straight root
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: No stable gravity direction means no bend.

markSchemeItems.text: The root in B would be horizontal or grow straight out; allow that it is not bent at all.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: Auxin is a plant hormone. Explain how auxin causes the results in apparatus A.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 6 shows apparatus A holding bean seedlings horizontally while remaining stationary; both apparatus were kept in the dark for 24 hours. Figure 7 shows the source drawing for the seedling from apparatus A before and after the 24-hour period. Auxin is a plant hormone. Explain how auxin causes the results in apparatus A.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Root auxin
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: gravity -> auxin lower side -> lower growth inhibited -> unequal elongation -> downward bend
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Unequal root growth produces the bend.

markSchemeItems.text: Auxin becomes unevenly distributed; allow more auxin on the lower side.
The upper side grows faster or more than the lower side; allow the lower side grows slower/less than the upper side.
Ignore saying there is more auxin on the upper side.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: Give two uses of auxin.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Farmers can use plant hormones to control the growth of plants. Give two uses of auxin.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Auxin uses
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: auxin -> weed/rooting/culture uses -> choose two
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Distinct practical uses score separately.

markSchemeItems.text: Auxin is used as a weed killer.
Auxin is used in rooting powders or to grow plants from cuttings.
Auxin is used to promote growth in tissue culture.
Allow auxin being used to inhibit lateral buds.
Award any two. For rooting powder, ignore an unqualified reference to shoots.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: A farmer sprayed an apple tree with gibberellin. Suggest two reasons why the farmer sprayed the apple tree with gibberellin.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Farmers can use plant hormones to control the growth of plants. A farmer sprayed an apple tree with gibberellin. Suggest two reasons why the farmer sprayed the apple tree with gibberellin.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Gibberellin uses
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: gibberellin -> flowering/fruit/ripening -> choose two
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Distinct crop-production effects score separately.

markSchemeItems.text: To promote, start, initiate or force flowering; allow growing more flowers.
To produce more fruit.
To produce bigger fruits.
To make the fruits ripen simultaneously.
Award any two. If neither more fruit nor bigger fruit is given, allow a bigger yield of apples.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: Suggest two ways to improve the students' method for measuring human reaction time.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Two students investigated human reaction time. Student A held a metre rule above student B's hand, then released it, and student B caught the rule as quickly as possible. Figure 8 shows the method and an example reading of 18 cm. Suggest two ways to improve the students' method for measuring human reaction time.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Reaction method
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: ruler method -> control/precision/repeats -> choose two
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Specific independent improvements score separately.

markSchemeItems.text: Use the same starting position for the ruler, for example with the ruler at 0 cm; other appropriate control variables are allowed.
Use a more precise scale, such as a ruler with a millimetre scale.
Repeat the measurement and calculate a mean, or repeat and eliminate anomalies.
Convert the measured distance to time.
Have student B rest their hand or arm on a table.
Award any two. Two valid control variables can earn both marks. Ignore 'more accurate scale'.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: Student B's reaction is coordinated by the nervous system. Give two ways that coordination by the endocrine system is different from coordination by the nervous system.
question.selfContainedPromptText: The human body has two coordination systems: the nervous system and the endocrine system. Student B's reaction in the metre-rule investigation is coordinated by the nervous system. Give two ways that coordination by the endocrine system is different from coordination by the nervous system.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Coordination contrast
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: endocrine vs nervous -> blood/chemical/slower/longer -> choose two
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Each point must clearly compare the systems.

markSchemeItems.text: Endocrine coordination travels via the blood, not via neurones/cells.
Endocrine coordination uses chemical transmission rather than electrical impulses/signals.
Endocrine coordination is slower than nervous coordination.
Endocrine effects are longer-lasting than nervous effects.
Award any two comparative differences; the converse is allowed if clearly referring to the nervous system. Ignore unqualified references to target organs.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: Name one hormone produced by gland A.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 9 shows endocrine glands in a female. The diagram labels the pituitary gland, adrenal gland and ovary, and marks two other glands as A and B. Name one hormone produced by gland A.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Thyroid hormone
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: thyroid gland -> thyroxine
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Link each gland to its hormone.

markSchemeItems.text: Thyroxine.
Do not accept thyroxide; ignore TSH or TRH.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: Name one hormone produced by gland B.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 9 shows endocrine glands in a female. The diagram labels the pituitary gland, adrenal gland and ovary, and marks two other glands as A and B. Name one hormone produced by gland B.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Pancreas hormones
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: pancreas -> insulin/glucagon
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Either blood-glucose hormone is valid.

markSchemeItems.text: Insulin.
Glucagon.
Award either accepted hormone.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: The adrenal gland produces the hormone adrenaline. Describe two effects of adrenaline on the human body.
question.selfContainedPromptText: The adrenal gland produces the hormone adrenaline. Describe two effects of adrenaline on the human body.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Adrenaline effects
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: adrenaline -> fight-or-flight changes -> choose two
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Distinct body changes score separately.

markSchemeItems.text: Increases heart rate; allow increased blood flow.
Increases breathing rate.
Increases oxygen delivery to cells, tissues or organs.
Increases glucose delivery to cells, tissues or organs.
Increases respiration or metabolism; allow increased energy release.
Increases sweating.
Dilates the pupils.
Causes vasoconstriction in the skin or digestive system, or vasodilation in skeletal muscles or the brain.
Raises blood glucose level or increases conversion of glycogen to glucose.
Increases blood pressure.
Slows digestion.
Award any two accepted effects. If no other marks are awarded, allow one mark for preparing the body for 'fight or flight'. Do not accept energy being produced, made or created.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: Hormones can be used for controlling human reproduction. Explain the use of hormones in: contraception; the treatment of infertility.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Hormones can be used for controlling human reproduction. Explain the use of hormones in contraception and in the treatment of infertility.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Reproductive hormones
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: contraception hormones -> inhibit FSH/LH -> no maturation/ovulation -> fertility hormones -> mature/release eggs
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Contrast prevention with stimulated fertility.

markSchemeItems.text: Level 3 (5–6 marks): Relevant reasons/causes are identified, given in detail and logically linked to form a clear account. Details of both contraception and infertility treatment are required.
Level 2 (3–4 marks): Relevant reasons/causes are identified and there are attempts at logical linking, but the resulting account is not fully clear.
Level 1 (1–2 marks): Points are identified and stated simply, but their relevance is not clear and there is no attempt at logical linking.
For contraception, oestrogen and progesterone, or progesterone alone, can inhibit FSH so a follicle/egg does not develop or mature and there is no egg to fertilise.
For contraception, hormonal treatment can inhibit LH so ovulation does not occur and there is no egg to fertilise.
Hormonal contraceptive methods include the oral pill, injection, implant, skin patch and hormone-containing IUD/IUS.
For infertility treatment, FSH stimulates maturation of several eggs/follicles, increasing the number of eggs matured.
LH stimulates ovulation and allows eggs/follicles to be collected, increasing the chance of fertilisation; IVF can then be followed by insertion of embryo(s) into the uterus.
Progesterone can maintain the uterus lining, increasing the chance of implantation.
Indicative content is not exhaustive. For Level 3, details of both contraception and infertility treatment are required.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: What is a dominant allele?
question.selfContainedPromptText: Some human disorders are inherited. Polydactyly is an inherited disorder in which a person has extra fingers or toes, and it is caused by a dominant allele. What is a dominant allele?

primaryAnswerChain.title: Dominance
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: one dominant copy -> phenotype expressed
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Dominance is expression in a heterozygote.

markSchemeItems.text: An allele that is always expressed in the phenotype, including when the other/recessive allele is present, in the heterozygote, or when only one copy is present.
Ignore saying that it is 'stronger'.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.6 Inheritance, variation and evolution.

question.promptText: Person 1 is heterozygous. Explain how Figure 10 shows that person 1 is heterozygous.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Polydactyly is caused by a dominant allele. Figure 10 shows its inheritance in one family. Use D for the allele for having polydactyly and d for the allele for not having polydactyly. Person 1 is heterozygous. Explain how Figure 10 shows this.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Heterozygote evidence
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: affected phenotype -> dominant allele -> unaffected child -> recessive allele passed -> heterozygous parent
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Use phenotype and offspring inheritance together.

markSchemeItems.text: Person 1 has polydactyly, so must have D/a dominant allele.
Person 1 has offspring person 5 without polydactyly, so person 5 is dd and must have inherited d from person 1.
An annotated genetic diagram can earn up to both marks.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.6 Inheritance, variation and evolution.

question.promptText: Persons 6 and 7 are expecting a fourth child. A doctor states that the probability of having a child with polydactyly is 0.5. Explain how the doctor determined this probability. You should: draw a Punnett square diagram; give the genotype of person 6 and the genotype of person 7; identify all the offspring that will have polydactyly.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Polydactyly is caused by a dominant allele. Figure 10 shows that person 6 has polydactyly, person 7 does not, and their first three children do not. Use D for the allele for having polydactyly and d for the allele for not having polydactyly. Persons 6 and 7 are expecting a fourth child. A doctor states that the probability of a child with polydactyly is 0.5. Explain how the doctor determined this probability by drawing a Punnett square, giving the genotype of each parent, and identifying all offspring with polydactyly.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Dominant cross
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: parent genotypes -> gametes -> Punnett combinations -> affected genotypes -> probability
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Derive probability from genotypes, not prior children.

markSchemeItems.text: Person 6's gametes are D and d (person 6 genotype Dd).
Person 7's gametes are d and d (person 7 genotype dd).
The Punnett derivation gives offspring genotypes Dd, Dd, dd and dd, consistently derived from the parental gametes.
Correctly identifies the two Dd offspring as having polydactyly, so half the offspring are affected.
Allow one mark for both sets of gametes if the parents are not identified. The final identification mark is awarded only if the offspring derivation is correct.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.6 Inheritance, variation and evolution.

question.promptText: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is another inherited disorder caused by a mutation. The mutation occurs in a gene called CFTR. For the CFTR gene, one allele in every 50 in the UK population is the cystic fibrosis allele. Explain why only one person in 2500 in the UK population has cystic fibrosis.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is an inherited disorder caused by a mutation in the CFTR gene. One allele in every 50 in the UK population is the cystic fibrosis allele. Explain why only one person in 2500 in the UK population has cystic fibrosis.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Recessive probability
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: recessive allele -> two copies needed -> one-copy chance -> multiply probabilities
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Two independent allele chances multiply.

markSchemeItems.text: The cystic fibrosis allele is recessive (CF is recessive).
A person must have two cystic fibrosis alleles to have cystic fibrosis.
The probability of inheriting one cystic fibrosis allele is 1/50.
The probability of inheriting two cystic fibrosis alleles is (1/50) × (1/50) = 1/2500.
Ignore merely repeating that the chance of one CF allele is one in 50, and ignore the unsupported statement 50 × 50 = 2500.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.6 Inheritance, variation and evolution.

question.promptText: Calculate the area of land needed to provide enough energy from maize seeds for 1000 chickens to grow to full size. Give your answer in m². Give your answer to 3 significant figures. 1 hectare = 10 000 m². 1 tonne = 1000 kg.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A farmer has 1000 chickens and feeds them maize seeds. One hectare produces 16.4 tonnes of maize seeds, the seeds contain 16 MJ per kg, chickens can use 80% of that energy, and each chicken needs 46 MJ to grow to full size. Calculate the required area of land in m² to 3 significant figures. Use 1 hectare = 10 000 m² and 1 tonne = 1000 kg.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Energy area
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: total demand -> yield and energy -> usable fraction -> area conversion -> required precision
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Keep units aligned before scaling land area.

markSchemeItems.text: Calculates a valid first intermediate, such as total chicken energy 46 000 MJ, maize mass per hectare 16 400 kg, usable energy fraction 0.8, or 12.8 MJ usable per kg.
Combines 46 000 MJ with 16 400 kg, or calculates 262 400 MJ energy per hectare before the 80% efficiency.
Calculates 209 920 MJ usable energy per hectare, or equivalently 3593.75 kg of maize required.
Converts to an area of 2191.3 m², for example (46 000 × 10 000) ÷ 209 920.
Gives 2190 m² to 3 significant figures.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.7 Ecology.

question.promptText: Another farmer produced 4200 kg of maize seeds in a field. The farmer fed the maize to 1000 chickens. At full size, the mean mass of one chicken was 2.2 kg. Complete Figure 11 to show a pyramid of biomass for the food chain from the maize seeds to 1000 chickens. You should: label the x-axis; use a suitable scale.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A farmer produced 4200 kg of maize seeds and fed them to 1000 chickens. At full size, the mean mass of one chicken was 2.2 kg. Complete Figure 11 to show a pyramid of biomass for the food chain from the maize seeds to the 1000 chickens, labelling the x-axis and using a suitable scale.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Biomass pyramid
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: consumer biomass -> symmetric scale -> biomass labels -> proportional bars
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Width shows biomass on a centred scale.

markSchemeItems.text: Use a suitable scale, symmetrical around 0.
Label the x-axis '(biomass in) kg' and label the bars maize and chickens.
Plot maize at 4200 kg and chickens at 2200 kg, within ±½ small square; ignore bar height.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.7 Ecology.

question.promptText: Chickens can use 80% of the biomass from the maize seeds they eat for respiration and growth. What happens to the remaining 20% of the biomass in the maize seeds?
question.selfContainedPromptText: Chickens can use 80% of the biomass from the maize seeds they eat for respiration and growth. What happens to the remaining 20% of the biomass in the maize seeds?

primaryAnswerChain.title: Biomass loss
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: undigested biomass -> gut remains -> egestion/faeces
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Unabsorbed food leaves through the gut.

markSchemeItems.text: It is lost via egestion/in the faeces.
Ignore urine, excretion, waste or 'not digested'. Do not accept respiration, not eaten, movement, heat or keeping warm.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.7 Ecology.

question.promptText: Which amino acids are found in significantly higher proportions in the QPM seeds? Tick (✓) one box.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Table 2 compares the mass of seven essential amino acids in normal maize and Quality Protein Maize (QPM), in g per kg of protein. Which amino acids are found in significantly higher proportions in the QPM seeds? Tick one box.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Significant increase
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: compare proportions -> clear increases -> paired selection
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Select the pair with clear proportional gains.

markSchemeItems.text: Lysine and tryptophan.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.7 Ecology.

question.promptText: Table 2 shows that 1 kg of QPM contains less leucine than 1 kg of normal maize. Suggest why a diet containing less leucine does not slow down the growth of chickens.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Proteins contain 20 amino acids. Chickens can make many of them from other dietary substances, while essential amino acids must be present in the diet. Table 2 shows that 1 kg of QPM contains less leucine than 1 kg of normal maize. Suggest why a diet containing less leucine does not slow down the growth of chickens.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Amino-acid sufficiency
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: lower dietary amount -> low requirement -> enough for growth
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Lower intake can still meet demand.

markSchemeItems.text: Chickens need only low amounts of leucine for growth.
Chicken proteins contain a low amount/proportion of leucine.
Award either answer. Do not accept saying that leucine is not needed for growth.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.7 Ecology.

question.promptText: Explain what is meant by the term 'homeostasis'.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Conditions inside and outside the human body often change. Homeostasis helps the human body to survive changing conditions. Explain what is meant by the term 'homeostasis'.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Homeostasis
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: internal conditions -> regulated -> optimum cell function
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Stable internal conditions support cells and enzymes.

markSchemeItems.text: Regulation, control or maintenance of internal conditions; allow keeping internal conditions the same.
Maintains optimum conditions for cells or enzyme activity; allow a description of optimum functioning of the cells/body.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: The kidneys have an important role in homeostasis. Describe what happens to glucose, protein and urea in the kidneys.
question.selfContainedPromptText: The kidneys have an important role in homeostasis. Describe what happens to glucose, protein and urea in the kidneys.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Kidney handling
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: glucose/urea filtered -> protein retained -> glucose reabsorbed -> urea excreted
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Filtration and reabsorption differ by substance.

markSchemeItems.text: Glucose and urea are filtered out of the blood.
Protein is not filtered out of the blood.
All glucose is reabsorbed back into the blood.
Urea is mostly not reabsorbed, or passes out in urine.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: Explain how ADH affects the production and concentration of urine by the kidneys.
question.selfContainedPromptText: The kidneys have an important role in homeostasis. Explain how ADH affects the production and concentration of urine by the kidneys.

primaryAnswerChain.title: ADH response
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: ADH rises -> tubules more permeable -> water reabsorbed -> less urine -> concentrated urine
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: More ADH conserves water.

markSchemeItems.text: An increased/high ADH level increases water reabsorption or increases permeability to water.
The water is reabsorbed from the kidney tubules.
Therefore increased ADH increases the concentration of urine.
Therefore increased ADH decreases the volume of urine.
Allow the converse explanation for decreased, low or no ADH.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.5 Homeostasis and response.

question.promptText: Explain how the use of GM soya bean plants and glyphosate can increase the yield of soya beans.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Glyphosate is a herbicide used in agriculture. Soya bean plants have been genetically modified (GM) to be resistant to glyphosate. A farmer can increase the yield of soya beans by growing GM soya bean plants and spraying glyphosate on the field. Explain how using both can increase the yield.

primaryAnswerChain.title: GM yield
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: resistant crop survives -> weeds killed -> less competition -> more resources -> more biomass
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Resistance removes competitors, freeing resources for growth.

markSchemeItems.text: Level 3 (5–6 marks): Relevant reasons/causes are identified, given in detail and logically linked to form a clear account. The answer must include detail of factors that increase yield.
Level 2 (3–4 marks): Relevant reasons/causes are identified and there are attempts at logical linking, but the resulting account is not fully clear.
Level 1 (1–2 marks): Points are identified and stated simply, but their relevance is not clear and there is no attempt at logical linking.
Glyphosate kills weeds but does not harm the GM soya bean plants.
There is less competition for light, water, ions/named mineral salts or space.
More light or water supports more photosynthesis, producing more glucose.
More glucose can be used in respiration for energy, cellulose for cell walls, starch for stored glucose/energy in soya beans, protein for cell structure/storage, or lipids for energy storage.
More water supports turgor/support, transport, a medium for reactions, and hydrolysis/digestion of stored organic substances.
More magnesium can be used to make chlorophyll.
More nitrate can be used to make amino acids/proteins.
More phosphate can be used to make DNA.

Indicative content is not exhaustive. The final Level 3 sentence in the official scheme says 'GM maize'; the question and all preceding scheme content concern GM soya bean plants, so this is treated as an obvious document wording inconsistency rather than different subject matter.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.6 Inheritance, variation and evolution.

question.promptText: Suggest two reasons why some people are concerned about the use of GM soya bean plants. Do not refer to ethical concerns or religion in your answer.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Soya bean plants have been genetically modified to resist the herbicide glyphosate. Suggest two reasons why some people are concerned about the use of GM soya bean plants. Do not refer to ethical concerns or religion.

primaryAnswerChain.title: GM concerns
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: GM crops -> health/spread/biodiversity/cost -> choose two
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Qualified non-ethical concerns score separately.

markSchemeItems.text: The effects or side effects on animals/humans when eaten are not known.
The gene/allele may be transferred to other wild plants.
Use of GM soya may reduce biodiversity.
Seed costs may increase for farmers, or the product cost may increase for consumers.
It may affect the flavour or taste of the product.
Award any two. Ignore ethical concerns or religion, and ignore an unqualified reference to cost.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.6 Inheritance, variation and evolution.

question.promptText: Value X is the number of tadpoles in sample 4, at 3 weeks. Calculate value X.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Table 1 gives the number of tadpoles in four samples at 0, 1, 2, 3, 5 and 8 weeks, with totals of 60, 58, 50, 32, 16 and 12. At 3 weeks the four sample entries are 9, 7, 10 and X. Value X is the number of tadpoles in sample 4, at 3 weeks. Calculate value X.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Missing total
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: known entries -> add known -> total minus sum -> missing value
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: The total reveals the unfilled entry.

markSchemeItems.text: Value X = 6.
If there is no answer on the final line, allow the answer entered in Table 1.

withheldQuestions.reason: Official prompt and mark evidence assess generic arithmetic, percentage work or graph construction; the surrounding Biology context alone is not enough to assign one curriculum chapter.

question.promptText: Complete Figure 4 to show how the total number of tadpoles changed over the 8 weeks. You should: label the x-axis; use a suitable scale for the x-axis; plot the data for the total numbers of tadpoles from Table 1; draw a line of best fit.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Table 1 gives total tadpole numbers of 60, 58, 50, 32, 16 and 12 at 0, 1, 2, 3, 5 and 8 weeks respectively. Complete Figure 4 to show how the total number of tadpoles changed over the 8 weeks. Label the x-axis, use a suitable x-axis scale, plot the Table 1 totals, and draw a line of best fit.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Graph completion
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: label and scale -> plot all points -> curved best fit
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Axes, points and trend earn separate credit.

markSchemeItems.text: Use a correct linear x-axis scale that occupies at least half the available space and label the axis 'weeks'.
Plot at least four of the six points correctly, within ±½ small square.
Plot all six points correctly, within ±½ small square (the second plotting mark).
Draw a curved line of best fit.
Ignore a line drawn point to point.

withheldQuestions.reason: Official prompt and mark evidence assess generic arithmetic, percentage work or graph construction; the surrounding Biology context alone is not enough to assign one curriculum chapter.

question.promptText: After 0 weeks, no more tadpoles hatched in the pond. Calculate the percentage of the tadpoles that would still be found in the pond at 4 weeks compared with 0 weeks. Use information from Figure 4.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Table 1 gives total tadpole numbers of 60, 58, 50, 32, 16 and 12 at 0, 1, 2, 3, 5 and 8 weeks. Figure 4 represents these totals against time in weeks with a curved line of best fit. After 0 weeks, no more tadpoles hatched in the pond. Calculate the percentage of the tadpoles that would still be found in the pond at 4 weeks compared with 0 weeks, using information from Figure 4.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Percentage remaining
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: read start -> read target -> target ÷ start -> times 100
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Use readings from the plotted trend.

markSchemeItems.text: Reads a correct value at 0 weeks and a correct value at 4 weeks from the student's line, for example 60 and 22, within ±½ small square.
Uses the correct percentage calculation, for example (22 ÷ 60) × 100.
Obtains 36.7%; allow 37% or 36.6 recurring, or a correct result using values from the student's graph.

If no line is drawn on Figure 4, allow a calculation using 60 and 24 (40%) for full marks. If a line is drawn but only table values 60 and 24 are used, only the calculation and result marks are available.

withheldQuestions.reason: Official prompt and mark evidence assess generic arithmetic, percentage work or graph construction; the surrounding Biology context alone is not enough to assign one curriculum chapter.

question.promptText: Calculate the ratio of chicken biomass to maize seed biomass. Use data from Question 07.2. Give your answer in its simplest form.
question.selfContainedPromptText: In Question 07.2, a farmer produced 4200 kg of maize seeds and fed them to 1000 chickens whose mean full-size mass was 2.2 kg. Calculate the ratio of chicken biomass to maize seed biomass and give it in its simplest form.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Biomass ratio
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: target biomass -> source biomass -> form ratio -> simplify fully
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Keep the requested order before simplifying.

markSchemeItems.text: Forms the biomass ratio 2200/4200 (chicken biomass to maize seed biomass).
Simplifies to 11:21; allow 0.5238…:1 or 1:1.9 with appropriate rounding.
